coverage_contract.problem_pattern: Determine whether one referenced object is to the left or right of another object in a natural image from a generic multiple-choice question.
supported_variations: Two distinct objects with clear spatial separation where the answer is left-versus-right relative position
Objects described by category and simple attribute cues such as color, with either object serving as the reference
unsupported_variations: Cases requiring depth, occlusion resolution, ambiguous group selection, or relations beyond simple horizontal left-right positioning
forbidden_case_specific_assumptions: Do not hardcode specific object pairs such as seesaw-vehicle or trash can-motorcycle
Do not rely on fixed image-side priors for particular categories or colors
Do not use one-off coordinates, memorized layouts, or dataset-specific camera framing assumptions
coverage_contract.why_this_should_generalize: The failures stem from not using any focused visual grounding. A reusable visual-focus primitive can help localize the two queried objects and compare their horizontal positions across many left-right relative-position questions in this family.

coverage_contract.problem_pattern: Questions ask for the color of a specific referenced object in an image, usually as multiple-choice direct attribute lookup requiring localized visual focus on that object.
supported_variations: Multiple-choice questions asking the color of a named object
Direct color lookup for a single salient or moderately localized object
unsupported_variations: Questions requiring counting, reading text, relational reasoning, or attributes beyond localized object color
forbidden_case_specific_assumptions: Do not hardcode colors associated with specific object types
Do not assume the target object is centered, largest, or uniquely salient
Do not use one-off image coordinates or case-specific regions
coverage_contract.why_this_should_generalize: Failures concentrate on missing localized color inspection, and the dominant family pattern is stable direct object-color identification across images, making a reusable color-focused primitive plus selection skill broadly applicable.
